stem: A utility company charges a flat monthly fee plus a per-unit rate for electricity usage. In January, a customer used 300 units and was charged $108. In February, the customer used 450 units and was charged $138. Starting in March, the company will increase the flat fee by 20% and decrease the per-unit rate by $0.02. If the customer wants the March bill to be exactly equal to the January bill, how many units of electricity can the customer use in March?
explanation: Let f be the flat fee and r be the per-unit rate. From January: f + 300r = 108. From February: f + 450r = 138. Subtracting the first equation from the second gives 150r = 30, so r = 0.20. Substituting back: f + 60 = 108, so f = 48. The new March rates are: flat fee = 48 × 1.20 = 57.60, and per-unit rate = 0.20 − 0.02 = 0.18. Setting the March bill equal to the January bill: 57.60 + 0.18u = 108. Solving: 0.18u = 50.40, so u = 50.40 ÷ 0.18 = 280.

distractorRationale.A: 210 results from incorrectly applying the 20% increase to both the flat fee and the per-unit rate instead of only the flat fee, while also ignoring the rate decrease. This gives a new rate of 0.20 × 1.20 = 0.24, and solving 57.60 + 0.24u = 108 yields 0.24u = 50.40, so u = 210.
distractorRationale.B: 252 results from correctly computing the new flat fee as 57.60 but forgetting to apply the $0.02 decrease to the per-unit rate, keeping it at 0.20. Solving 57.60 + 0.20u = 108 gives 0.20u = 50.40, so u = 252.
distractorRationale.C: 280 is correct. The new flat fee is 48 × 1.20 = 57.60 and new rate is 0.20 − 0.02 = 0.18. Solving 57.60 + 0.18u = 108 gives u = 280.
distractorRationale.D: 300 results from assuming the customer can use the same number of units as in January without recalculating under the new rate structure, ignoring that the changed fee and rate require a different usage level to produce the same bill.
